content.problemStatement:
Chef has two permutations AAA and BBB of length NNN. Here, a permutation of length NNN is an array containing each integer from 111 to NNN exactly once.

Chef wants to transform array AAA into array BBB using the following operation any number of times (possibly zero):


Choose an index kkk (1‚â§k‚â§N1 \le k \le N1‚â§k‚â§N) and either increase AkA_kAk‚Äã by 111 or decrease AkA_kAk‚Äã by 111.
However, after every operation (including the initial and the final array), the following condition must always hold:

Aj‚âÝAj+1for all 1‚â§j<NA_j \ne A_{j+1} \quad \text{for all } 1 \le j < NAj‚ÄãÓÄÝ=Aj+1‚Äãfor all 1‚â§j<NIntermediate values may become any integers (they do not have to remain in [1,N][1, N][1,N], and AAA does not have to remain a permutation), but the final array must be exactly equal to BBB.

Determine whether it is possible to transform AAA into BBB while maintaining the adjacency constraint at all times.

Print "YES" if it is possible, otherwise print "NO".


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT ‚Äî the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of:

A line containing the integer NNN.
A line containing NNN space-separated integers representing the permutation AAA.
A line containing NNN space-separated integers representing the permutation BBB.




Output Format
For each test case, print "YES" if it is possible to transform AAA into BBB while always maintaining the adjacency constraint; otherwise print "NO".


Each character of the output may be printed in either uppercase or lowercase, i.e. the strings `NO`, `No`, `nO`, and `no` will all be treated as equivalent.
constraints: 1‚â§Ai‚â§N1 \leq A_i \leq N1‚â§Ai‚Äã‚â§N for all 1‚â§i‚â§N1 \leq i \leq N1‚â§i‚â§N
1‚â§Bi‚â§N1 \leq B_i \leq N1‚â§Bi‚Äã‚â§N for all 1‚â§i‚â§N1 \leq i \leq N1‚â§i‚â§N
The sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 2‚ãÖ1052 \cdot 10^52‚ãÖ105

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: We already have A=BA = BA=B, and 1‚âÝ21 \ne 21ÓÄÝ=2, so the adjacency rule is satisfied.
No operations are needed, and the answer is YES.
Test case 222: A=[1,2]A = [1, 2]A=[1,2] and we want B=[2,1]B = [2, 1]B=[2,1].
It can be proved that no matter how we operate on A1A_1A1‚Äã and A2A_2A2‚Äã, if we want to end up with the array [2,1][2, 1][2,1] we must have A1=A2A_1 = A_2A1‚Äã=A2‚Äã at some point of the process.
So, it's impossible to reach BBB, and the answer is NO.
